coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of green dye-sensitized solar cell based on ZnO nanoparticles as a photoanode and graphene quantum dots as a photo-sensitizer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zamiri, Golnoush
Bagheri, Samira
coredata.dc:description: Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) consist of single- or few-layer graphene with a size less than 20 nm and stand for a new type of QDs with unique properties combining the graphene nature and size-resulted quantum effects. GQDs possess unique optical and electronic properties, and in particular possess a band-gap less than 2.0 eV because of quantum confinement and edge effects. In this study, we investigated the performance of DSSCs using different thicknesses of ZnO nanoparticles as a photo-anode and GQDs as a green photosensitizer. The current voltage (I-V) test results indicate that the performance of DSSCs is improved by increasing the thickness of the photo-anode and the thickness of 40 μm shows the highest efficiency for DSSC device based on ZnO nanoparticles photo-anodes. The DSSC using ZnO nanoparticles as a photo-anode with thickness of 40 μm shows almost same efficiency when we replaced N-719 with GQDs which is confirmed that using GQDs as an alternative to ruthenium based dyes is a new approach for DSSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Green photosensitizer
Dye-densitized solar cell
Solar cell fabrication

(a) Photoluminescence and (b) UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs.

(a) I-V and (b) IPCE Curves of DSSCs using ZnO photo-anodes with different thicknesses and GQDs as a photosensitizer.

(a) I-V and (b) IPCE curves of DSSCs devices based on ZnO nanoparticles using N-719 and GQDs as photosensitizer.

Photovoltaic characteristics of DCCSs devices based on ZnO nanoparticles with different thicknesses and using GQDs as a photosensitizer.
Photovoltaic characteristics of DCCSs devices based on ZnO nanoparticles using N-719 and GQDs as photosensitizer.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of green dye-sensitized solar cell based on ZnO nanoparticles as a photoanode and graphene quantum dots as a photo-sensitizer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology & Catalysis Research Centre (NANOCAT), IPS Building, University of Malaya, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) consist of single- or few-layer graphene with a size less than 20 nm and stand for a new type of QDs with unique properties combining the graphene nature and size-resulted quantum effects. GQDs possess unique optical and electronic properties, and in particular possess a band-gap less than 2.0 eV because of quantum confinement and edge effects. In this study, we investigated the performance of DSSCs using different thicknesses of ZnO nanoparticles as a photo-anode and GQDs as a green photosensitizer. The current voltage (I-V) test results indicate that the performance of DSSCs is improved by increasing the thickness of the photo-anode and the thickness of 40 μm shows the highest efficiency for DSSC device based on ZnO nanoparticles photo-anodes. The DSSC using ZnO nanoparticles as a photo-anode with thickness of 40 μm shows almost same efficiency when we replaced N-719 with GQDs which is confirmed that using GQDs as an alternative to ruthenium based dyes is a new approach for DSSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) was first reported by O’Regan and Grätzel in 1991, is an attractive solar energy conversion technology which is a promising alternative for silicon-based solar cells because of their low cost of manufacture, easy fabrication procedures, modifiable aesthetic features such as colour and transparency and respectable energy conversion efficiency [1].

Having acceptable conversion efficiency increases the chance for the DSSCs to compete with commercially available, but expensive solar cells based on silicon or compound semiconductors [2]. Nevertheless, the increased additional conversion efficiency has been restricted by energy loss due to the recombination between electrons and either the oxidized dye molecules or electron-accepting species in the electrolyte during the charge-transport process [3–5]. Such a recombination is predominately derived from the lack of a depletion layer on the TiO2 nanocrystallite surface, and becomes significantly serious when the thickness of the photoelectrode film increases. In order to understand this, DSSC technology based on ZnO has been explored extensively. A promising alternative to TiO2 for the fabrication of DSSC photo-anode is ZnO [6–9].

For many years, ruthenium-based complexes were the “champion” dyes of DSSCs, due to they usually show high efficiency (10–11%) due to their wide absorption range from the visible to the near-infrared (NIR) regime [10,11]. However, despite the fact that higher power conversion efficiencies and relatively stable DSSCs have been fabricated using ruthenium sensitizers, ruthenium is not an earth abundant material. Moreover, some ruthenium complexes show broad absorption spectra, they generally have modest molar extinction coefficients [12,13], which limit their performance when employed to sensitize thin electrodes.

Zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) consist of single- or few-layer graphene with a size less than 100 nm and stand for a new type of quantum dots (QDs) with unique properties combining the graphene nature and size-resulted quantum effects. GQDs possess unique optical and electronic properties, and in particular possess a bandgap less than 2.0 eV because of quantum confinement and edge effects [5–7]. Various interesting optoelectronic properties of GQDs have been recently revealed and their potential application in cell imaging, bio-sensing, and solar cells has been explored. GQDs also have rich functional groups, such as carboxylic acid moieties at the edge, thus imparting them with excellent water solubility and subsequent functionalization possibilities [8].

In this research, we intend to increase the performance and efficiency of DSSCs by replacing the dye with GQDs as a photo-sensitizer and also demonstrate the time of electron injection from GQDs into the conduction band of the ZnO.

All chemicals were used without further purification. Graphite powder, H2SO4, H3PO4, KMnO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, KCl, ZnO and Triton-X100 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ruthenium polypyridyl dye N-719, was purchased from Solaronix and used as a photosensitizer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare GQDs, the first step is to synthesis graphite oxide from graphite using the modified Hummer’s method [14].

Graphene oxide [15] has been obtained by oxidizing graphite flakes with sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), and potassium permanganate (KMnO4). The mixture will be stirred to complete the oxidation of graphite. The formed graphite oxide will be washed thrice with HCl using centrifuge, and repeatedly washed with deionized water until its pH was ∼4–5. During the washing process with deionized water, the graphite oxide underwent exfoliation, which results in the thickening of the GO solution and the formation of GO gel [14].

GQDs are prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method [16]. GO (prepared by Hummers method) will be added into the acid under refluxing. This solution will be then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. Then, the mixture will be subjected to a hydrothermal process in a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to obtain the GQDs solution [17]. The solution will be ultrasonicated and subsequently filtered.

The preparation of DSSCs is crucial for the advancement of DSSC technology. Each of its components is capable of influencing the PEC parameters. The quality of materials, variation of the spacer distance between the photo-anode and the counter electrode, and quality of electrical contacts on the electrodes can result in different values of short-circuit photocurrents and photo-voltages. Both physical and chemical engineering should be optimized to obtain highly efficient devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZnO coating on FTO glass by Dr. Blade method
ce:section.ce:para: FTO-glass was cut into 2×2 cm strips, the FTO slides were cleaned by dipping it in detergent solution for 15 min, then sonicated 5 min each time in acetone, ethanol, and distilled water. ZnO pastes were prepared by adding 1 g ZnO in 5 mL of absolute ethanol, 2 mL of Triton-X100, and 0.5 mL of distilled water, and stirred for 20 min at room temperature. After that, the ZnO paste was deposited onto a cleaned FTO-glass using the doctor-blade technique. The thickness of the subsequent films can be altered using multiple layer of scotch tape masks and additional ZnO pastes after its annealed at 450 °C for 30 min. After annealing, the ZnO film has cooled for 15 min at room temperatures.

To fabricate of DSSCs, the surface-treated ZnO were immersed in ethanol solutions of ruthenium-based dye and GQDs at room temperature to allow for sufficient dye absorption. Platinum-coated FTO glass was used as the counter electrode, prepared by placing a drop of platinum-ethanol solution onto the clean FTO glass substrate, and subsequently sintered. The DSSC was sandwiched between the ZnO nanoparticles paste-coated FTO glass (anode) and the platinum coated onto FTO glass (cathode) and an ionic liquid electrolyte Z-100 was injected between two electrodes.

The presence of various functional groups in the GO and GQDs were measured using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Model: Bruker IFS 66/S). The morphology was studied by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) Hitach, Japan. The UV–vis spectroscopy was performed using Hewlett Packard HP-8453 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence spectroscopy was performed using Hewlett Packard HP-8453 spectrophotometer. The thickness of photoanodes was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) Hitach, Japan. The photovoltaic performances of DSSCs were characterized by a Solar simulator with AM 1.5. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) measurement was conducted with Photovoltaic SR_EQE_IQE_Mapping.

The FTIR spectra of GQDs and GO are shown in Fig.1 (a). Each GQD show defects due to incomplete reduction. FTIR proved the presence of OH, epoxy/ether, CO; it is assumed that these groups caused the structural defects. The peak at 1741 cm−1 belongs to CO, while the peaks at 1259 and 1370 cm−1 correspond to epoxy/ether groups, making GQDs water soluble. The peak centered at 1620 cm−1 is assigned to CC stretching, and the broad range from 2500 to 3500 cm−1 confirms the presence of COOH. The characteristic band position at 3386 cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching [18]. Fig.1(b) shows HRTEM image of GQDs. The prepared GQDs have a quite uniform size of around 5 nm.

In Fig.2 (a) an excitation-dependent PL spectrum was obtained from GQDs, while two peaks at 430 nm and 560 nm were observed with 321 nm excitation. The PL spectrum was dominated by the peak at 560 nm, which is responsible for green fluorescence of GQDs [18]. Fig.2(b) shows that GQDs efficiently absorb UV light. A typical absorption peak at 321 nm was observed, which is similar to the reported GQDs. The peak at 321 nm represents the uniform sp2 clusters in GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of DSSCs based on TiO2 photo-anode
Effect of different immersion time in GQDs on PCE in DSSCs based on ZnO photo-anode
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of different immersion times on the performance of DSSCs, ZnO photo-anodes with thickness of 10 μm were immersed in GQDs for 7, 14, 21, and 28 h. Fig. 3 compares the I-V characteristics obtained from DSSCs made of ZnO nanoparticles as photo-anodes, which were immersed in GQDs for different hours. After increasing the immersion time from 7 h to 21 h, the efficiency was increased from 0.133 to 0.6%.

As seen Fig. 3 and Table 1 , we note that increasing the immersion time of ZnO photo-anodes in GQDs improves the performance of DSSCs. More photosensitizer molecules can be adsorbed when immersion time is increased, which also increase the light harvesting efficiency [19]. DSSCs devices based on ZnO nanoparticles photo-anodes were immersed in GQDs at (7, 14, 21 and 28 h). The lowest immersion time (i.e., 7 h) resulted in the lowest efficiency (PCE = 0.133%). As the duration of immersion time increased from 7 h to 21 h, the ISC increased from 0.361 mA to 1.29 mA, while PCE increased from 0.133% to 0.6%. However, when the immersion time was increased from 21 h to 28 h, ISC and PCE decreased from 1.29 to 1.068 mA and from 0.6% to 0.435%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of ZnO photo-anode with different thicknesses on PCE in DSSCs using N-719 as photosensitizer
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of the thickness of ZnO photo-anodes when N-719 is used as a photosensitizer in DSSCs, different layers of ZnO nanoparticles were coated onto FTO glasses to prepare photo-anodes, which were then immersed in N-719 for 21 h [15]. The FESEM images in Fig.4 (a) show that the thickness of each layer of TiO2 nanoparticles is ∼10 μm.

Fig.5 (a) compares the I-V characteristics obtained from DSSCs from different thicknesses of ZnO nanoparticles coated onto the FTO glasses. As seen in Fig.5(a), when the thickness increase from 10 μm to 40 μm, the efficiency increased from 0.396 to 1.13%.

The effect of the thickness of ZnO on the performance of the DSSCs was examined (Fig.5(a) and Table 2 ). The results show that increased thickness of ZnO nanoparticle film improves the performance of DSSCs. The thicker ZnO active layer suggests that more photosensitizer molecules can be adsorbed, which results in increased light harvesting efficiency [20]. We prepared DSSC with ZnO nanoparticles films that were 10 μm, 20 μm, 30 μm, 40 μm and 50 μm thick. Table 2 and Fig.5(a) indicated that the thinnest ZnO film (i.e., 10 μm) reported the lowest efficiency (PCE = 0.396%). As the thickness increased from 10 μm to 40 μm, ISC increased from 1.122 mA to 2.657 mA, while PCE increased from 0.396% to 1.13%. However, when an even thicker ZnO nanoparticle film was used (i.e., 50 μm), ISC and PCE decreased from 2.657 to 2.54 mA and from 1.13% to 1.04%, respectively. The decrease in ISC and PCE can be rationalized as follows.

The IPCE plots in Fig.5(b) showed that the active photon-to-current responses of ZnO photo-anode with a thickness of 40 μm are more red-shifted compared to the other thicknesses of ZnO photo-anode (10, 20, 30 and 50 μm), which indicates that the spectral absorption range is effectively broadened [21]. On the other hand, the maximum IPCE value of 11% was obtained at around 500 nm, which is related to ZnO nanoparticles photo-anode with a thickness of 40 μm. These results suggest that light harvesting is significantly improved by increasing the thickness of the ZnO photo-anode [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of ZnO photo-anode with different thicknesses on PCE in DSSCs using GQDs as photosensitizer
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of ZnO photo-anodes thickness when GQDs is used as a photosensitizer in DSSCs was investigated by coating different layers of ZnO nanoparticles onto FTO glasses to prepare photo-anodes, which was then immersed in GQDs for 21 h.

Fig.6 (a) compares the I-V characteristics obtained from DSSCs from different thicknesses of ZnO nanoparticles coated onto FTO glasses. Clearly, after increasing the thickness from 10 μm to 40 μm, the efficiency increased from 0.6% to 1.26%.

The effect of the thickness of ZnO on the performance of the DSSC was examined (Fig.6(a) and Table 3 ). The increasing thickness of ZnO nanoparticle film improves the performance of the DSSCs [22]. The thicker ZnO active layer suggests that more photosensitizer molecules can be adsorbed, which increases the light harvesting efficiency [20]. DSSCs were prepared with ZnO nanoparticle films that were 10 μm, 20 μm, 30 μm, 40 μm and 50 μm, which was then immersed in GQDs for 21 h. As seen in Table 3, the thinnest ZnO film (i.e., 10 μm) reported the lowest efficiency (PCE = 0.6%). As the thickness increased from 10 μm to 40 μm, ISC increased from 1.29 mA to 3.169 mA, while PCE increased from 0.6% to 1.26%. However, when an even thicker ZnO nanoparticle film was used (i.e., 50 μm), PCE decreased from 1.26% to 1.21%, respectively.

The maximum IPCE value of ∼50% was obtained at ∼530 nm, which is due to the ZnO photo-anode that was 40 μm thick (Fig.6(b)). As seen in Fig.6(b), the IPCE results indicate that light harvesting is significantly improved by increasing the thickness of ZnO photo-anode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of DSSCs based on 4-Layes ZnO photo-anode using GQDs as a photosensitizer and N-719 as a photosensitizer
ce:section.ce:para: Fig.7 (a) shows the vacuum energy level of ZnO nanoparticles, N-719, and GQDs. According to Fig.7(a), the energy level of the ZnO conduction band is −4.05 eV, while the GQD represent −3.82 eV and N-719 is −3.01 eV.

Fig.7(b) indicates the PL spectra of photo-anodes based on ZnO nanoparticles immersed in N-719 and GQDs as photosensitizers under 325 nm laser excitation. ZnO will absorb the incident photons with sufficient energy equal to or higher than the band-gap energy, which will produce photoinduced charge carriers [23]. Broad peaks with maximum emissions at around 563, 565, and 571 nm can be observed for ZnO coated on FTO glass, and ZnO photo-anodes immersed in N-719 and GQDs, respectively [24,25].

The IPCE plots in Fig.8(b) clearly shows that the active photon-to-current responses of ZnO photo-anode immersed in GQDs are more red-shifted compared to the ZnO photo-anode immersed in N-719, which indicates that the spectral absorption range is effectively broadened. However, the IPCE value of ZnO photo-anode immersed in GQDs exceeds 52%, while the quantum efficiency is reduced to 11% for the DSSC device based on ZnO nanoparticles using N-719 as a photosensitizer [26].
The EIS for DSSCs based on ZnO nanoparticles using N-719 and GQDs as photosensitizers measured in dark conditions were recorded in a frequency range between 0.01 Hz and 100 kHz and shown in Fig. 9 .

In dark condition, the three semicircles located in high, middle, and low frequency regions (left to right) of Nyquist plots are attributed to the redox reaction at the Pt counter electrode and the electron transfer at the ZnO/dye/electrolyte interface and charge transfer in the electrolyte [27]. They are, respectively, assigned to the electro- chemical reaction at the Pt counter electrode (Rct1) and chemical capacitance (CPE1), the charge transfer at the ZnO/dye/electrolyte (Rct2) and chemical capacitance (CPE2) and the charge diffusion process of I−/I3 − ions (Zw). Compared with the ZnO immersed in N-719, ZnO photoanode immersed in GQDs shows significant reduction on Rct2 which means that using GQDs as a photosensitizer makes the electron transfer easier at the ZnO/dye/electrolyte [28].

In this article, the performance of low cost DSSC with improved PCE based on ZnO nanoparticles as a photo-anode and GQDs as a photosensitizer were investigated experimentally. To investigate the effect of different immersion times on the performance of DSSCs, ZnO photo-anodes were immersed in different photosensitizers for different times. For both photosensitizers, The I-V tests and IPCE results confirm that the highest efficiency is related to DSSC when immersion time is 21 h due to more photosensitizer molecules can be adsorbed when immersion time is increased, which also increase the light harvesting efficiency. The effect of ZnO photo-anode thickness on DSSCs performance was studied by coating multiple layers of ZnO nanoparticles onto the FTO glasses and then immersed in N-719 and GQDs as photosensitizers. For both photosensitizers, the I-V tests and IPCE results confirm that photo-anodes with 40 μm thicknesses show the highest efficiency due to with increasing thickness, more photosensitizer molecules present in the semiconductor layer for absorbing sunlight. However, increasing thickness requires a longer path for the photo generated electrons to reach the working electrode, which increases the rate of electron recombination. Therefore, the current started decreasing post-optimal thickness. To compare the efficiency of DSSCs using GQDs as a photosensitizer with DSSCs using N-719 as photosensitizer, ZnO photo-anode with 40 μm thicknesses were immersed in both photosensitizers for 21 h. The I-V test shows that the efficiency of ZnO photo-anode immersed in GQDs is higher than the efficiency of ZnO photo-anode immersed in N-719, due to the conduction band of GQDs being closer to the conduction band of ZnO compared to N-719. The IPCE results confirm all these findings. The DSSCs using ZnO as a photo-anode with thickness of 40 μm shows higher efficiency when we replaced N-719 with GQDs which is confirmed that using GQDs as an alternative to ruthenium based dyes is a new approach for DSSCs.
